old_claim.note: Scientific Societies provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Scholasticism is the relevant shared theological-dialectical context for Tridentine dogmatic debate and clerical education; it is not a scientific-society institution.

source_shape.source_locator: Scholarship Challenges Tradition contrasts humanist theology with scholastic professional theology, while later sections connect Counter-Reformation controversy to Protestant and humanist criticism; this supports scholasticism as shared theological context rather than scientific societies.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source reviews humanist, scholastic, Protestant, and Counter-Reformation controversy around theology and textual criticism.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The evidence supports a theological method and institutional-learning context, so the edge is common_dependency and replaces the unrelated scientific-societies edge.

ontology_before: The Counter-Reformation depended on Scientific Societies, conflating Catholic doctrinal reform with later scientific inquiry organizations.
ontology_after: The Counter-Reformation depends on Scholasticism as shared theological-dialectical context, while scientific societies are excluded from the direct prerequisite set.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: counter_reformation now depends on scholasticism and no longer directly depends on scientific_societies.
would_reject_if: A source showed scientific societies were an institutional driver of the Council of Trent or Catholic reform.
The Counter-Reformation node were rescoped to early modern learned societies rather than Catholic reform and doctrinal response.
A dedicated Council of Trent, Jesuit order, or Tridentine seminary node were introduced and should replace this broader scholasticism context.
short_reason: Counter-Reformation theology fits scholastic context better than scientific-society institutions.
